title: Quantum Algorithm Researcher - Research Lab or Quantum Startup
setting: A research group or startup exploring algorithms for chemistry, optimisation, or quantum simulation
companySize: Research team of 6-20 within a lab or company of 40-200 people
description: You spend your days translating hard problems into quantum-friendly forms, testing algorithm ideas, and deciding whether claimed speedups survive real hardware and resource limits. The work is mathematical, experimental, and often more sceptical than the outside world expects.

schedule.activity: Read recent papers and review the current benchmark target
schedule.note: The first question is usually whether the problem formulation still makes sense.

schedule.activity: Prototype a circuit or variational workflow
schedule.note: Most promising ideas become debugging sessions before they become results.

schedule.activity: Discuss resource estimates with hardware and software teammates
schedule.note: The algorithm only matters if the hardware could plausibly run it.

schedule.activity: Compare noisy simulation output to a classical baseline
schedule.note: Much of the day is spent disproving weak claims.

schedule.activity: Write a note, internal memo, or paper draft summarising the findings
schedule.note: Clear sceptical writing is part of the job.

responsibilities: Map domain problems into quantum representations
Estimate circuit depth and hardware feasibility
Compare quantum proposals to strong classical baselines
Communicate which ideas are promising and which are not
challenges: Working with hardware that is still noisy and resource-limited
Explaining nuanced results to audiences who want simple breakthroughs
rewards: Working at the frontier of computation and physics
Contributing to how the field judges real value
careerPath: PhD or research engineer -> Quantum algorithm researcher -> Senior researcher -> Research lead or chief scientist
